mnemonics.title: RAM - Rapid Access Memory
mnemonics.content: Think of RAM as the 'Rapid Access Memory' - it gives fast access to data that changes frequently. Remember: R-A-M = Rapid Access Memory (even though technically it stands for Random Access Memory, this mnemonic helps recall its speed)

mnemonics.title: ROM - Ready Only Memory
mnemonics.content: ROM is 'Ready Only Memory' - it comes ready with pre-stored data that cannot be easily changed. Remember: R-O-M = Read Only Memory - the data is there when you need it, ready to read

mnemonics.title: Register Types Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: ACCUMULATOR: A Holds Results - Accumulator stores arithmetic results | PROGRAM COUNTER: PC Tracks Next Instruction - PC keeps track of the next instruction address | MEMORY ADDRESS REGISTER: MAR Marks Address Location - MAR holds the address | MEMORY DATA REGISTER: MDR Moves Data Readily - MDR holds data being transferred

mnemonics.title: Memory Hierarchy Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: From fastest to slowest: CPU Registers → Cache → Main Memory (RAM) → Secondary Storage. Use: 'Registers Cache Main Storage' (R-C-M-S) or imagine a pyramid with CPU at apex

mnemonics.title: Volatile vs Non-Volatile
mnemonics.content: RAM is Volatile (Vanishes When Power Off) | ROM is Non-Volatile (Never Forgets) - Remember: RAM = Random Access Memory but also 'Rapidly Awaits Maintenance' (forgot) - it loses data when power is cut

acronyms.usage: Used for primary memory that can be read and written; data is lost when power is off (volatile memory)
Used for permanent storage of firmware and boot instructions; data remains even without power (non-volatile)
The main processor that executes instructions; contains registers for temporary storage
Holds the memory address from which data will be fetched or to which data will be stored
Holds the data that is being transferred to or from memory
Contains the address of the next instruction to be executed
Register used to store intermediate results of arithmetic and logical operations
Type of RAM that uses flip-flops; faster but more expensive; used in cache memory

acronyms.expansion: Type of RAM that uses capacitors; needs periodic refresh; slower but cheaper; used in main memory

visualTips.visualization: Imagine an inverted pyramid: At the top (smallest, fastest) = CPU Registers | Middle = Cache | Bottom (largest, slowest) = RAM | Base = Secondary Storage. Think of water flowing down - data flows through this hierarchy
Picture the CPU as a small office desk, and registers as the top surface where you immediately place papers you're working on - fastest access, limited space
Imagine RAM as your desk surface - you can spread out many papers (data/programs) here, work with them quickly, but when you turn off the lights (power), everything gets cleared
ROM is like a printed book - the content is permanently written, you can only read it, and it stays intact even when stored for years without power

visualTips.concept: Data Flow between CPU and Memory
visualTips.visualization: Think of MAR as the address you write on an envelope, MDR as the envelope itself holding data, and the address bus as the postal system that delivers it to the correct mailbox (memory location)

analogies.analogy: Think of registers as the notepad in your hand while doing calculations - you can instantly write and read numbers here, but you can only hold a few at a time
RAM is like a teacher's desk - you can place many books and papers here to work with, rearrange them easily, but at the end of the day (power off), everything gets cleared for the next day
ROM is like a printed textbook or a carved stone inscription - the information is permanently fixed, you can read it anytime, and it stays there forever without any power source

Volatile memory (RAM) is like your short-term memory - it holds information only while you're awake (power on) | Non-volatile memory (ROM) is like long-term memory stored in books - it lasts even when you're asleep (power off)
Cache is like a small drawer next to your main desk - smaller than the desk (RAM) but closer to your hand (CPU), so you can grab frequently used items faster
Secondary storage (HDD/SSD) is like a library or warehouse - huge capacity but takes time to retrieve items, used for permanent storage of all your files and programs
